planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring or fall when the soil is cool and moist; Astilbe chinensis is more drought-tolerant and summer-heat-tolerant than most astilbes, making it a better performer in gardens south of zone 6 where the classic A. x arendsii hybrids often struggle.

directions: Plant in partial shade in consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil — Astilbe chinensis tolerates more sun and drier conditions than most astilbes but performs best with afternoon shade and reliable moisture. The finely cut, fern-like foliage is attractive throughout the growing season and the plume-like flower spikes in mauve-pink to rosy-purple bloom later in summer than most astilbes, extending the shade garden's color season. After flowering, the dried seed heads persist attractively into winter and provide texture and wildlife food. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in early spring to maintain vigor — as the center of the clump dies out, division is needed to rejuvenate it. Unlike many moisture-loving shade plants, established plants have fair tolerance for summer dry spells.
sources: American Horticultural Society A-Z Encyclopedia of Garden Plants
Pennsylvania Horticultural Society Gold Medal Award notes

planting.note: Plant in spring in a moist, sheltered spot; rodgersia requires consistently moist to wet soil and is often at its best beside ponds, streams, or in boggy woodland areas. Emerging leaves in spring are vulnerable to late frosts — site in a sheltered location or protect with fleece.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich, fertile, slightly acidic soil; rodgersia is a bold, architectural shade perennial with enormous horse-chestnut-like compound leaves up to 24 inches across that emerge bronze-tinged in spring and deepen to dark green through summer. The fluffy astilbe-like flower plumes in creamy white to pink rise dramatically above the foliage in midsummer. It is one of the most dramatic foliage plants available for wet, shaded locations — particularly impressive at the edge of a pond or naturalistic woodland garden where its scale commands attention. The roots are massive and rhizomatous; once established, plants resent disturbance. In southern gardens (zone 7–8) it benefits from consistently moist soil and afternoon shade to prevent leaf scorch in summer heat.
sources: RHS Award of Garden Merit plants list
Darke and Hoffman: The American Woodland Garden, Timber Press 2002

planting.note: Plant in spring in its permanent location — this species has an extensive deep root system and resents transplanting once established; smilax is exceptionally well-adapted to wet, acidic, shaded sites in the Gulf Coast South where it grows naturally in swamp margins and wet woodland.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in moist to wet, acidic, sandy or peaty soil; laurel-leaf greenbrier is an evergreen native vine of the Gulf Coastal Plain that uses tendrils to scramble through shrubs and trees, providing year-round cover and valuable black berries for wildlife from fall through winter. The long, lance-shaped, leathery leaves remain glossy dark green through winter, making it valuable for screening and structure in year-round southern gardens. It tolerates flooding, deep shade, and sandy coastal conditions. New shoots in spring are edible and have been used by Native Americans as a vegetable and starch source. The dried rhizomes yield a starchy food and historically formed the basis of a root beer-style drink. Use on fences and naturalized areas where it can spread without becoming a maintenance challenge.
sources: University of Florida IFAS — Florida Native Landscaping
Native Plants for Southeastern Gardens — Elmore and Halfacre, University of Georgia Press

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall or early spring, just below the soil surface, in deep shade with consistent moisture; seed germination requires cold stratification and is extremely slow. Himalayan mayapple emerges earlier in spring than the American species and the foliage is more ornamental — deeply lobed with attractive purplish-brown mottling.

directions: Plant in deep, moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in full to partial shade; Himalayan mayapple is a collector's shade perennial prized for its deeply lobed, marbled leaves with intricate purplish brown and green mottling that emerge in spring with dramatic effect. The single white flower with a pink flush sits at the leaf junction and develops into an ovoid red fruit that is edible when fully ripe — the only edible part of the plant. All other parts contain potent podophyllotoxin, the basis of several cancer chemotherapy drugs, and are toxic. In cultivation P. hexandrum is generally grown as an ornamental curiosity rather than as a fruit plant. It forms spreading colonies in ideal conditions, going fully dormant in midsummer. Closely related to American mayapple (P. peltatum) but considerably more ornamental.
sources: National Center for Biotechnology Information — podophyllotoxin research

planting.note: Plant after all frost danger passes in partial to full shade — impatiens is the classic full-shade annual for continuous bloom in zones 3–11. In the Gulf Coast, it performs best in fall through spring, as it sulks in peak summer heat; treat as a cool-season annual in zone 9 for best results.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil; impatiens is the most widely planted shade annual in the world, producing a nearly uninterrupted mass of flowers in white, pink, salmon, red, coral, lilac, and bicolored shades from planting through first frost. It requires consistent moisture — plants wilt dramatically when dry but recover quickly if watered before severe wilting. The fungal disease downy mildew (Plasmopara obducens) devastated standard impatiens populations starting around 2011; when purchasing, choose disease-resistant SunPatiens or impatiens 'Beacon' series rather than older unselected varieties, or ensure plants are certified downy mildew-resistant. Self-sowing is uncommon; collect seeds in late summer before pods explode. Excellent for window boxes, containers, and massed plantings under trees where few other flowering plants thrive.
sources: American Society for Horticultural Science — Impatiens downy mildew resistance research
North Carolina State University Extension Floriculture

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a consistently moist, sheltered spot with afternoon shade; ligularia is reliably dramatic in wet woodland gardens and pond-side plantings but will show severe wilting in any hot, dry afternoon sun — this is a diagnostic stress response, not permanent damage, but indicates a site change is needed.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in consistently moist to wet, fertile, humus-rich soil; ligularia is a bold, dramatic shade perennial with large, rounded to kidney-shaped, dark green (or in the 'Britt Marie Crawford' and 'Desdemona' cultivars, deep purple-brown) leaves that may reach 20 inches across. The bright orange-yellow daisy-like flowers atop tall stems create vivid color in the shade garden in midsummer. Ligularia performs best beside ponds, streams, or rain gardens where its thirst for moisture is constantly satisfied; in an ordinary border it requires regular watering and will wilt on hot afternoons even when the soil is moist. The purple-leaved forms are among the most ornamentally striking shade plants available. Slugs are a significant pest; manage with copper tape barriers or targeted baits.
sources: RHS Award of Garden Merit plants list
Hillier Manual of Trees and Shrubs
